coredata.dc:title: pH-sensitive nanocarrier based on gold/silver core–shell nanoparticles decorated multi-walled carbon manotubes for tracing drug release in living cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Peng
Wang, Zhuyuan
Zong, Shenfei
Zhu, Dan
Chen, Hui
Zhang, Yizhi
Wu, Lei
Cui, Yiping
coredata.dc:description: We fabricate a multifunctional nanocarrier based on multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) decorated with gold/silver core–shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag NPs) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) for tracking the intracellular drug release process. In the demonstrated nanocarrier, the Au@Ag NPs adsorbed on the surface of MWCNTs were labeled with the pH-dependent SERS reporter 4-Mercaptobenzoic acid (4MBA) for SERS based pH sensing. FITC was conjugated on MWCNTs to provide fluorescence signal for tracing the MWCNTs. Fluorescent doxorubicin (DOX) was used as the model drug which can be loaded onto MWCNTs via π–π stacking and released from the MWCNTs under acidic condition. By detecting the SERS spectrum of 4MBA, the pH value around the nanocarrier could be monitored. Besides, by tracing the fluorescence of FITC and DOX, we can also investigate the drug release process in cells. Experimental results show that the proposed nanocarrier retained a well pH-sensitive performance in living cells, and the DOX detached from MWCNTs inside the lysosomes and entered into the cytoplasm with the MWCNTs being left in lysosomes. To further investigate the drug release dynamics, 2-D color-gradient pH mapping were plotted, which were calculated from the SERS spectra of 4MBA. The detailed release process and carrier distribution have been recorded as environmental pH changes during cell endocytosis. Furthermore, we also confirmed that the proposed nanocarrier has a good biocompatibility. It indicates that the designed nanocarrier have a great potential in intraceable drug delivery, cancer cells imaging and pH monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS
Fluorescence
Carbon nanotubes
Dox
pH sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERS pH sensing using the fMWCNT
Intracellular pH sensing of a single cell
DOX loading and extracellular release from nanocarrier
Intracellular tracking drug release from nanocarrier and the cytotoxicity in vitro

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SERS spectra of fMWCNTs inside HeLa cells placed in pH 3.0 and 8.0 buffer solutions. (b) pH calibration curve of the intensity ratio of the two bands from HeLa cells incubated with fMWCNTs in different pH value buffer solutions.

Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with fMWCNTs. (A) Bright field image (B) fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells. (C) Fluorescence imaging of lysosomes which were stained with Lyso-Tracker Red. (D) Overlay of (B) and (C). (Scale bar 20μm).

The bright field image of HeLa cells incubated with fMWCNTs for (A) 2h, (C) 6h, (E) 24h. The SERS mapping image of the ratio of the SERS intensity at 1390cm−1 and 1183cm−1, (B) 2h, (D) 6h, (F) 24h. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM images of cellular uptake of fMWCNTs and their cellular localizations in HeLa cells (Arrows indicate fMWCNTs).

Confocal fluorescence images of the DOX release from fMWCNTs-DOX inside the cell for different times. (A) SERS imaging of fMWCNTs-DOX inside the cells. (B) Fluorescence imaging of DOX released inside the cells. (C) Fluorescence imaging of lysosomes which are stained with Lyso-Tracker Green. (Scale bar 10μm).
The synthesis and functionalization procedure of multifunctional nanocarrier based on MWCNTs.

head.ce:title: pH-sensitive nanocarrier based on gold/silver core–shell nanoparticles decorated multi-walled carbon manotubes for tracing drug release in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Photonics Center, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We fabricate a multifunctional nanocarrier based on multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) decorated with gold/silver core–shell nanoparticles (Au@Ag NPs) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) for tracking the intracellular drug release process. In the demonstrated nanocarrier, the Au@Ag NPs adsorbed on the surface of MWCNTs were labeled with the pH-dependent SERS reporter 4-Mercaptobenzoic acid (4MBA) for SERS based pH sensing. FITC was conjugated on MWCNTs to provide fluorescence signal for tracing the MWCNTs. Fluorescent doxorubicin (DOX) was used as the model drug which can be loaded onto MWCNTs via π–π stacking and released from the MWCNTs under acidic condition. By detecting the SERS spectrum of 4MBA, the pH value around the nanocarrier could be monitored. Besides, by tracing the fluorescence of FITC and DOX, we can also investigate the drug release process in cells. Experimental results show that the proposed nanocarrier retained a well pH-sensitive performance in living cells, and the DOX detached from MWCNTs inside the lysosomes and entered into the cytoplasm with the MWCNTs being left in lysosomes. To further investigate the drug release dynamics, 2-D color-gradient pH mapping were plotted, which were calculated from the SERS spectra of 4MBA. The detailed release process and carrier distribution have been recorded as environmental pH changes during cell endocytosis. Furthermore, we also confirmed that the proposed nanocarrier has a good biocompatibility. It indicates that the designed nanocarrier have a great potential in intraceable drug delivery, cancer cells imaging and pH monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, multifunctional drug delivery systems have been one of the research hot spots in the field of nanomedicine as they bear a variety of advantages, such as transporting drugs effectively to the target tumor with less damages to normal cells (Liu et al., 2011; Timko et al., 2010). Among those systems, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are well known as a drug delivery system owing to the relatively large surface area and unique chemical property (Ali-Boucetta et al., 2008; Berlin et al., 2011; Kostarelos et al., 2009; Ren et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2005). Moreover, when CNTs are functionalized with designed biomolecules or nanoparticles, they will not only obtain a good biocompatibility and a precisely tumor cell targeting ability, but also a special optical property for tracing and photo thermal therapy (Bianco et al., 2005; Herrero et al., 2011; Lu et al., 2012; Nair et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2012). For instance, some researchers have reported that DOX-CNT based drug delivery shows excellent cytotoxicity and high efficiency for in vitro cancer cell treatment (Kang et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2009; Lu et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2009).

In a specific CNT-based drug delivery system, cancer drugs are usually bonded to CNTs either covalently or non-covalently. And the drug release is usually controlled by a trigger (Gu et al., 2011; Herrero et al., 2009; Samori et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2009). For example, DOX can be easily attached and released from the surface of CNTs by controlling pH-dependent π–π stacking force between DOX and CNTs at pH 8.0 and 5.0.

In order to study intracellular drug delivery and release process of the afore mentioned CNT-based drug delivery systems, molecules or nanoparticles with special functions are immobilized on CNTs to form hybrid materials for dynamic tracing. In this tracing, various methods such as fluorescence (Kang et al., 2012; Nair et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015) and surface enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) (Song et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2013) have been widely used for cellular imaging and tracing. However, SERS and fluorescence have some intrinsic disadvantages. For example, fluorescence has intrinsic limitations of fluorescence overlap and SERS is limited in high-speed analysis due to its relatively long acquisition time. Thus, multi-mode imaging is considered essential for dynamic tracing (Chen et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2012).

Besides, many biological processes are accompanied with pH changing, such as endocytosis (Liu et al., 2007; Pallaoro et al., 2010). To further understand the dynamic process of nanocarriers in living cells, it is necessary to take pH sensing into consideration for monitoring the drug delivery process.

Herein, in this work, a tri-mode multifunctional MWCNTs based drug nanocarrier is fabricated, which can realize fluorescence imaging, SERS imaging and pH sensing. The structure of the multifunctional nanocarrier is presented in Scheme 1. To fabricate the presented MWCNTs based nanocarrier, first, a cationic polymer PAH are used to wrap the carboxylated MWCNTs by electrostatic interaction to turn the zeta potential of MWCNTs from negative to positive and provide MWCNTs with amino groups. Second, the Au@Ag NPs, which served as SERS-active substrate for SERS pH sensing and imaging, are adsorbed onto the MWCNTs by electrostatic interaction. Third, the fluorescent dye FITC is conjugated to MWCNTs covalently for fluorescence imaging (Kang et al., 2012) and the 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4MBA) is labeled with Au@Ag NPs on MWCNTs for pH sensing The terminal carboxyl group of 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4MBA) exhibits the COO− form in alkaline solutions and its SERS spectrum changes accompanied with pH changing (Pallaoro et al., 2010; Talley et al., 2004). Finally, doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) is loaded onto the surface of MWCNTs via pH-dependent π–π stacking interaction. It is worth mentioning that the fabricated hybrid nanocarrier has excellent performances in investigating both DOX release process and intracellular DOX distribution by tracing the fluorescence, SERS signal of DOX and nanocarrier and intracellular pH values.
All the materials and experimental procedures were presented in the Supplementary materials.

In order to functionalize MWCNTs with Au@Ag nanoparticles, the pretreatment of MWCNTs is necessary. Otherwise the hydrophobic graphitic surface of pristine MWCNTs will result in large aggregations during nanoparticles assembly as previous reported (Correa-Duarte et al., 2004; Grzelczak et al., 2007; Jiang et al., 2003; Singhal et al., 2011). In our experiment, the pristine MWCNTs with length less than 2μm were curled up or twisted together to form an intertwined shape as shown in Fig. S1A, while the oxidized MWCNTs were 0.1–0.5μm in length and disperse well in solution as depicted in Fig. S1B. As shown in the inset of Fig. S1B, the oxidized MWCNTs have a good solubility in water with deep black color while the pristine MWCNTs cannot disperse in water and sink to the bottom.

The TEM images of oxidized MWCNTs after Au@Ag assembly are presented in Fig. S1C, respectively. As can be seen in the inset of Fig. S1C, Au@Ag NPs with a typical size of 28nm are uniformly adsorbed onto MWCNTs surface in Fig. S1C. To further demonstrate the adsorption of Au@Ag NPs, UV–vis spectra of both Au@Ag NPs and MWCNTs/Au@Ag NPs in solution are performed in Fig. S1E, bare Au@Ag NPs display a typical absorption peak of Au@Ag NPs at around 400nm. But this peak of MWCNTs/Au@Ag NPs was red-shifts to 435nm and a broader full width at half maximum (FWHM) was appeared, which indicate that Au@Ag NPs are deposited onto the surfaces of MWCNTs. This phenomenon has also been observed by Zhao et al. (2008). The EDX spectrum in Fig. S1D provides further evidence of the composition of MWCNTs/Au@Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS pH sensing using the fMWCNT
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the pH-sensitivity of as-prepared fMWCNTs, we exploit the SERS spectra features of 4MBA molecules. As depicted in Fig. S2A, the peaks at 1077 and 1590cm−1 are assigned to the breathing modes of benzene ring attribute to 4MBA and the peaks at 1183cm−1 are the ring modes of adsorbed benzoate species (Kwon et al., 1994). These peaks are relatively not sensitive to the environmental pH changes. In comparison, the peak at 1390cm−1, which is assigned to the COO− stretching mode (Pallaoro et al., 2010; Talley et al., 2004), quite sensitive to pH as the ionization of COOH to COO− is vigorous around the pKa value of 4MBA (about 4.63 for 4MBA). And this is the reason why we use 4MBA to decorate MWCNTs in this work.

Therefore, a ratio between peak intensity at 1390cm−1and 1183cm−1 can be exploited to profile the pH-dependent SERS performance of fMWCNTs. Fig. S2B plots this pH calibration curve at pH range from 3.0 to 8.0. It indicates that the fMWCNTs can be served as a good pH sensor for investigation cell physiological activities.

To demonstrate the performance of this composite nanoparticle for intracellular pH sensing, HeLa cells are incubated with fMWCNTs and then their culture media are replaced with different pH buffer solutions for SERS measurement. Two typical SERS spectra of HeLa cells in pH 3.0 and pH 8.0 buffer solutions are shown in Fig. 1A, the aforementioned ratio between the peak intensity at 1390cm−1 and 1182cm−1 indicates that fMWCNTs retains a strong pH-sensitivity after being incorporated in HeLa cells. For pH values between 3.0 and 8.0, the calibration curve is plotted in Fig. 1B, which shows a similar trend as that in pure buffer solutions in Fig. S2B. This result predicts that this CNT-based probe can realize its pH sensing ability for studying actual cellular processes.

To confirm their sensing activity inside cells, we record the SERS spectrum of cytoplasm region in HeLa cells, as previous studies indicated that functionalized MWCNTs will enter the lysosomes after being taken up by cells (Kang et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011). In this work, it can be clearly seen from bright field microscopic image (shown in Fig. S3A) that fMWCNTs has successfully entered into HeLa cells. To investigate the process of fMWCNTs entering lysosomes, we then merge this image with fluorescence images of lysosomes stained with Lyso-Tracker Red (Fig. S3B). A labeled area was chosen (Fig. S3C) for SERS measurement. The SERS spectrum recorded in Fig. S3D presents a specific intensity ratio of 1390cm−1/1182cm− 1 of fMWCNTs probe under acidic pH condition. This result demonstrates that fMWCNTs is able to trace actual cellular processes with pH changes, such as entering lysosomes. Furthermore, by tracing the fluorescence of FITC on fMWCNTs, a series of fluorescence images at different depths of a cell incubated with fMWCNTs were obtained (shown in Fig. S4). The fluorescence of fMWCNTs was observed in cytoplasm region without into the nucleus which indicated that fMWCNTs has successfully incorporated into the cells.

Besides pH sensing, it is worth mentioning that this typical probe has the potential for further fluorescence and SERS dual-mode imaging. In our experiment, fluorescent molecules FITC are immobilized on the surface of MWCNTs by covalently bonding to the amine groups of PAH wrapped on MWCNTs surface (Wu et al., 2014).Then fMWCNTs are incubated with HeLa cells for 5h before the fluorescence and SERS imaging.

Bright field, fluorescence and SERS images of HeLa cells are presented in Fig. S5 respectively. As shown in Fig. S5A, the black dots inside cells indicate that probes have been incorporated directly into cells. Moreover, the merged confocal microscope image of FITC fluorescence (Fig. S5B) and SERS spectrum of 4MBA (Fig. S5C). Fig. S5D shows that the fluorescence and SERS signals almost overlap with each other, which confirms that the fMWCNTs can be used as a dual-mode optical probe for cellular imaging.

To demonstrate trace ability of the probes after coupling with FITC, after incubation HeLa cells with fMWCNTs, the lysosome is stained with Lyso-Tracker Red. By merging the fluorescence images of HeLa cells ( Fig. 2B) and lysosomes (Fig. 2C), it is found that the fluorescence of FITC overlaps well with that of the lysosomes (Fig. 2C). This implies that fMWCNTs have been incorporated into lysosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular pH sensing of a single cell
ce:section.ce:para: Another application of this CNT-based probe is the real time monitoring of some cellular processes. We elucidate this technique by recording the dynamic process of fMWCNTs occupying lysosomes within a time range of 24h. As depicted in Fig. 3, this technique is based on mapping the pH sensitive intensity ratio of SERS peaks at 1390 and 1183cm− 1 as mentioned in Fig. 1B. First, the strong intensity peak of the benzene band at 1070cm−1 (Fig. S2A) is utilized to confine the SERS image area and the 1070cm− 1 SERS band intensity below threshold were set to black. Second, the corresponding environmental pH value of every spot can be obtained from the pH-ratio calibration curve in Fig. 1B. Finally, a vivid view of this dynamic process is gained after replacing calculated pH values with four colors.

As shown in Fig. 3B, after 2h incubation, fMWCNTs probes start to enter into HeLa cells via endocytosis as some dark red color spots emerges which corresponds to acidic pH environment inside lysosomes. After 6h incubation, the amount of neutral pH color inside the cell gradually decreases (Fig. 3D), which reflects that more carriers have been swallowed by HeLa cells. Finally, most spot measured are dark red and related to an acidic pH condition after 24h of incubation. This phenomenon indicates that the nanocarriers are finally trapped inside lysosomes and this phenomenon is consistent with other studies using carbon nanotubes for drug delivery (Kang et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2009).

To further demonstrate the accuracy of our real time tracing of probes, cellular TEM images are taken at 2h, 6h and 24h after probe incubation and are presented in Fig. 4. Accordingly, it is found that the intracellular uptake starts when fMWCNTs get access to HeLa cells and quite few particles penetrate into cells within 2h period. With incubation time increased from 2h to 6h (Fig. 4B) and finally to 24h (Fig. 4C), the amount of intracellular fMWCNTs gradually outweighs those extracellular ones and almost all of them are restrained in the cytoplasm region after 24h, in consistent with the real time monitoring achieved from Fig. 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading and extracellular release from nanocarrier
ce:section.ce:para: For drug delivery, a commonly anticancer agent DOX is chosen by conjugating DOX onto the surface of fMWCNTs via pH-dependent π–π stacking (Ali-Boucetta et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2009).The absorption spectrum of DOX loaded fMWCNTs is shown in Fig. S6A, the typical DOX absorption peak also emerge in DOX loaded fMWCNTs but red-shift from 488nm to around 550nm compared with free DOX sample, indicating the loading of DOX onto fMWCNTs. Fig. S6B presents fluorescence of these two samples. After been loaded, the fluorescent of DOX is quenched due to Forster resonance energy transfer between DOX and fMWCNTs. This fluorescent quenching also proves that DOX has been loaded onto the surface of fMWCNTs (Qian et al., 2014a, b).The SERS spectra of nanocarriers with or without DOX are also given in Fig. S6C. Compared with SERS spectrum of fMWCNTs, two new bands at 452cm− 1 and 1261cm−1 emerge after DOX loading, which are in consistent with characteristic SERS spectrum of DOX (Huang et al., 2013; Nabiev et al., 1991; Yang et al., 2014). In this experiment, the weight percentage of DOX loaded on MWCNTs could reach above 90% when 0.5mg MWCNTs was loaded with 0.5mg DOX.

In order to realize pH-responsive DOX release process, two PBS buffer solutions at pH=5.0 and pH=7.4 are served as solvent to prove that DOX could be released from fMWCNTs via pH-responsive π–π stacking interaction under acidic conditions (Kang et al., 2012). Fig. S7 shows the time-dependent DOX release from nanocarrier at different pH buffer solutions. By measuring the UV–vis absorption of DOX in dialysate, it turns out that most DOX are still attached to the surface of fMWCNTs and only less than 5% of DOX are released from the fMWCNTs in pH=7.4 PBS buffer solution even after 48h. However, when the pH of the buffer solution was adjusted to 5.0, the percentage of DOX released from fMWCNTs sharply increases to about 30% within 10h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular tracking drug release from nanocarrier and the cytotoxicity in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: For further portraying and understanding of intracellular DOX release of the fMWCNTs-DOX, HeLa cells are cultured with this fMWCNTs-DOX and monitored under a confocal laser scanning microscope for 6h. Considering that the DOX fluorescence is at around 580nm, a green emissive dye is used to stain lysosomes after HeLa cells being incubated with fMWCNTs-DOX. We note that when DOX is released from the fMWCNTs, the quenched fluorescent emission of DOX will recover. Therefore, DOX release can be monitored from its fluorescent signals. Thus, an integration of fluorescence and SERS information of both stained lysosomes and DOX could reflect the dynamic DOX release process within 6h time range. In brief, during a certain time period, for instance 1h, 2h and 6h in our situation, all three signals match well with each other in Fig. 5D, I, N respectively, indicating that DOX release do take place in lysosomes. In time change, a comparison between every column images of a certain measurement (fluorescence of DOX and dye, SERS of 4MBA) reveals the evolution of DOX release. As more and more nanocarriers enter and DOX release proceeds, the sharp edge of lysosomes inside HeLa cells are well defined from the fluorescence and SERS signals and overlap with images from bright field microscopy. After 1h incubation as shown in Fig. 5A–E, the fMWCNTs-DOX have not internalized into the cells. Only a few nanocarriers went inside the cells,so less DOX was released from MWCNTs, and a weak red fluorescence appeared near the location of nanocarriers. Then after 2h incubation as shown in Fig. 5F–J, the number of nanocarriers inside cells was increased and SERS signals overlaped with the green fluorescence of the lysosome. Meanwhile, more fluorescence of DOX appeared inside cells which indicated that more free DOX were released from the fMWCNTs-DOX. As shown in Fig. 5K–O, when the nanocarriers were incubated with cells for 6h, a higher SERS intensity of the nanocarriers was observed inside the cytoplasm, and almost the whole cell was stained red due to the fluorescence of DOX. This means that after incubation for 6h, the SERS intensity of nanocarriers gradually increased. Red fluorescence of DOX was also increased because more free DOX were released from fMWCNTs inside the lysosomes under acidic environment, and the release DOX molecules entered into the cytoplasm. These results are partially consistent with the previous reports of CNT-based drug delivery system (Liu et al., 2009).

Finally, viability of HeLa cell is investigated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S8, the fMWCNTs without DOX shows no obvious cytotoxicity to HeLa cells even at a relatively high concentration of 5μg/mL. However, after DOX loading, the viability of HeLa cells is effectively reduced to about 50% after 24h. Considering that the fMWCNTs have a good biocompatibility, this reduction in viability is obviously attributed to the DOX released into HeLa cells. Therefore, we conclude that this probe have a great potential in drug delivery and related optical studies.

We have fabricated a multifunctional fMWCNTs which shows excellent performance in monitoring nanocarrier endocytosis dynamics and drug release process. The fMWCNTs has favorable SERS-fluorescence signals under laser excitation inside cells. The combined SERS-fluorescence spectroscopy obtained demonstrate that during DOX delivery and release, DOX pre-loaded on as-prepared fMWCNTs is released inside lysosomes and finally moves into the nucleus, while fMWCNTs are still inside the lysosomes. Besides the SERS-fluorescence image tracing, pH-dependent 4MBA molecules are chosen to functionalize the MWCNTs with additional pH sensitivity. Our results have proved that SERS signals of fMWCNTs are capable of detecting pH variation during intracellular process such as endocytosis. Therefore, we have developed a real-time technique to monitor the distribution of fMWCNTs in cells and the uptake pathway by 2D pH related signal intensity ratio mapping. We anticipate that this fMWCNTs have certain potential applications in optical imaging, biosensing and controllable drug delivery in cancer therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Key Basic Research Program of China (Grant no. 2015CB352002), the Excellent Youth Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20140023), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Nos. 61535003, 61275182, 61177033, 61505027), The Innovation Fund of School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University (2242015KD002), Science Foundation for The Excellent Youth Scholars of Southeast University, the Scientific Research Foundation of Graduate School of Southeast University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.09.002.
